title: ProtocolLab TLS Performance Contract Architecture
purpose: Describe the separation of TLS handshake lifecycle, resumed-session provenance, record workload, execution metadata, and performance evidence.
design_summary: Reusable closed TLS profile identities isolate protocol version, cipher, and peer-authentication differences. Separate lifecycle scenarios cover fresh and resumed handshakes, accepted and rejected 0-RTT, KeyUpdate, and a profile-driven record direction/size matrix. Load profiles remain scenario-neutral.
data_and_state_considerations: Resumption evidence must bind each resumed connection to a successfully established source session. Certificate material and session secrets are never public artifacts. Record payloads use deterministic generator and SHA-256 metadata.
edge_cases_and_constraints: TLS 1.2 is compatibility-only and cannot be aggregated with TLS 1.3.
Accepted and rejected 0-RTT are distinct outcomes and use replay-safe fixtures only.
Record coverage specifies plaintext sizes, not TLS record segmentation.
Hardware acceleration differences make otherwise valid results non-comparable.
alternatives_considered: Treating TLS as only an HTTP execution flag was rejected because handshake and record performance are independently valuable.
Combining full, resumed, and 0-RTT handshakes was rejected because their security and lifecycle semantics differ.
risks: Certificate-chain generation can dominate setup unless it is outside the measured window.
Session caches can produce false resumed-handshake claims unless acceptance is proven.
